01 / 13 رقص
RAQS رقص

A Couture Experience

Lahore Fort Courtyard
One Night
One Collection
One Argument
02 / 13

The Premise

This is not
a fashion show.

RAQS is a live couture experience staged in the courtyard of Lahore Fort. A Pakistani couture collection moves through performance, classical dance, and light. The clothes are not presented to an audience. They are worn by women who are already inside a world the audience has walked into. The show lasts approximately one hour and fifteen minutes. Nothing about it resembles a conventional runway.

03 / 13

The Problem

Pakistani couture exists at a world-class level. It is not being seen that way.

The current runway format, borrowed wholesale from western fashion week conventions, does not do justice to what this craft actually is. A white-lit stage and a straight walk communicate nothing about embroidery that takes six hundred hours, or about the women these clothes were made for, or about the civilisation they come from.

The format has not caught up with the ambition. RAQS proposes a different format entirely, one rooted in where this fashion actually comes from.

04 / 13

The Concept

Women taking space.

The collection traces the arc of a woman's life. Learning, becoming, choosing, arriving. Each act corresponds to a stage of that arc, expressed through silhouette, classical dance, music, and light. The audience does not watch the clothes. They witness the women wearing them, moving through a fort that was not built for them, filling it anyway.

4

Acts

~75

Minutes total

150

Guests maximum

05 / 13

The Acts

Story first. Couture second.
Choreography serves both.

The dance is never competing for attention. It opens the emotional door for what comes next. Each act: the dancer establishes the mood, reaches the final pose, goes still. Silence. Then in another part of the courtyard, a single spotlight finds the model. The dancers become architecture. The model is the only thing moving.

Act I

Seekhna

to learn

Two kathak performers. One pool of light. They reach the final pose and freeze. Silence. A spotlight finds the first model in another part of the courtyard. She is the only thing moving. The room has nowhere else to look.

Act II

Banana

to become

The kathak duo remains, now frozen like stone. Sufi dervishes enter the raised alcoves for the first time, spinning at the periphery. The model owns the open centre ground. The whirling frames her without competing.

Act III

Chunna

to choose

More kathak performers join. More sufis on the platforms. Every dancer from Acts I and II remains in the space, still. Lighting shifts mid-act from warm to cool. The model walks through an overwhelming quiet.

Act IV

Hona

to be

Every performer moves again simultaneously. All the stillness breaks at once. Full illumination. Multiple models enter one by one. An hour of building. This is the release. The courtyard is completely full.

0 — 20 min

Arrival

Courtyard already alive. Archive booklets on seats. Live musician in a corner.

20 — 60 min

The Four Acts

8 to 10 minutes per act. 2 minute pause between. Performers accumulate and hold position.

60 — 75 min

Close

Full courtyard. All motion returns. All models. The night ends in fullness and light.

06 / 13

The Lighting Arc

The courtyard starts in near darkness.
Light is earned.

The illumination mirrors the arc of the collection. Every act brings more light into the courtyard, not as a production choice but as a narrative one. By Act IV the fort is finally, fully seen. The architecture that has been holding the night reveals itself only at the end.

Act I

·

Seekhna

A single pool of light on the dancer. The rest of the courtyard is dark. The audience hears before they see.

Act II

· ·

Banana

A few diyas become visible at the edges. The space begins to reveal its shape.

Act III

· · ·

Chunna

Lanterns begin to glow around the arched architecture. The fort walls come into view.

Act IV

· · · ·

Hona

Full courtyard illumination. The audience sees the complete space for the first time. The fort is finally, entirely present.

07 / 13

The Arrival and The Archive Booklet

Guests arrive to a world that is already alive.

The courtyard is lit and inhabited before a single guest takes their seat. A live musician plays in a corner. Miniature paintings from the collection's visual world are on carved side tables. The archive booklet is already waiting on every seat. Nobody is waiting for anything to start. It has already started.

The Invitation

Sent by hand, sealed in aged paper. For select international press, an audio invitation arrives instead. A deliberately crackling recording that feels like a transmission from another time. The voice gives only a date, a location, and one line: aa jao.

The Archive Booklet

Not a programme. Not a playbill. A field journal printed on aged paper, bound with thread. A pencil is attached. Guests are observers. The pencil reinforces that.

Inside

The four acts, each introduced by a single line of Urdu poetry.

A short note from the creative director.

Small illustrations and sketches of the collection.

Space left blank for handwritten observations.

Party Favour

Guests leave with a lookbook designed like a vintage desi newsletter. Each look photographed. Below each: what to listen for. A raga. A verse. A word.

For influencers, this is the asset that travels.

Hand invitation sealed in aged paper Audio transmission for press Archive booklet with pencil Vintage lookbook newsletter
08 / 13

The Collection

16 looks.
Mughal-rooted.
Internationally staged.

Heavily embellished couture referencing the visual language of the subcontinent. Each look is named. Each name carries the emotional register of its act. The clothes are armour as much as they are beauty. They are not styled to recede. They move.

Zardozi

Gold and silver thread embroidery. The most labour-intensive craft in the collection. Each piece between three and six hundred hours.

Kaamdaani

Flat metal wire worked into fabric by hand. Produces a shimmer that reads differently in candlelight than in daylight.

Mirror work

Shisha embroidery from Sindh. The mirrors catch the fort's torchlight during the show, creating movement the fabric alone cannot.

Gulkari

Flower-based surface embellishment in silk thread. Softness against weight is the tension the collection lives in.

09 / 13

The Campaign Film

The show is also the shoot.

Shot in the same fort on a separate night. The film opens with young girls learning to dance, follows them through movement, through joy, through the claim of space, and ends where the show begins. A woman in couture, fully arrived. Non-commercial treatment. Cinematic. Shot on film where possible. One production, two permanent assets.

Film Structure

Opening — girls learning to dance
Middle — growing, choosing, moving
Close — a woman in couture, fully arrived
End frame — the fort courtyard, empty, waiting

10 / 13

Press and Audience Strategy

Build the noise at home first.
Let it travel outward.

International media does not arrive because they are invited. They arrive because they feel they cannot afford to miss what is already being talked about. The strategy works in two phases: establish RAQS as an unmissable cultural moment in Pakistan first, then activate international press through that momentum.

Phase One — Pakistani Press

Dawn Images

First access. Exclusive preview feature two weeks before the show covering the concept, the archive booklet, and the creative direction. Sets the cultural context domestically.

Instep, The News

Interview with the creative director. Focus on the decision to stage fashion as performance, and what that says about where Pakistani couture is going.

Paperazzi, Images on Sunday

Guest list and atmosphere coverage. The visual world of RAQS enters Pakistani cultural conversation before the night itself.

Diva Magazine, SHE Magazine

Fashion editorial tie-in. Looks from the collection shot in the fort in the week before the show. Published day-of as contextual coverage.

Phase Two — Digital and Influencer

Pakistani digital fashion voices

A curated group of Pakistani creators with genuine fashion audiences, not just reach, receive the audio invitation and the archive booklet ahead of the show. Their content creates visible anticipation online before the night.

No phone policy, announced publicly

The announcement itself generates conversation. The decision signals that RAQS is an experience to be witnessed, not documented. It creates curiosity about what a dedicated content team will release after.

Content release in three waves

Teaser before. Three-minute highlight film within 48 hours. Full campaign film one week later. Each wave creates a new press moment, keeping RAQS in conversation for two weeks after the show.

Phase Three — International Outreach

Vogue Arabia, Vogue India

Direct outreach begins six weeks before the show with a physical press package, the concept deck, fabric swatches from the collection, and a personal note from the creative director. Follow-up with the audio invitation. Exclusive early access to the campaign film offered in exchange for coverage.

Business of Fashion, i-D

Pitched as a story about format, not just fashion. The angle: Pakistani couture is creating a new grammar for how a show can work. The Pakistani press coverage and digital noise from Phase One and Two is shared as proof of cultural resonance before they commit.

South Asian fashion diaspora press

Brown Girl Magazine, Wore It Well, and South Asian fashion writers with established international readership are contacted directly. They bridge the gap between Pakistani cultural context and international fashion audience.

11 / 13

Content Strategy and Metrics

Two weeks before

The build

Dawn Images exclusive drops. Audio invitation clips released to select digital media. Fort preparation content seeded across brand channels. No phone policy announced publicly, creating anticipation for the official content release.

Within 48 hours

The highlight film

A curated three-minute highlight film released simultaneously across all channels. Photographed look cards released individually over 48 hours. Influencer content goes live at the same time. Pakistani press reviews publish.

One week later

The campaign film

Full campaign film released as a standalone piece with its own press moment. Physical lookbook newsletter mailed to international media contacts. The show becomes a film. The film lives indefinitely.

Success looks like

Coverage in at least two international publications
Campaign film exceeding 500K views in first week
Measurable collection enquiries from non-Pakistani buyers
RAQS referenced as a format, not just an event

What this changes

Pakistani couture enters international critical conversation
The show format becomes replicable and scalable
The designer holds a campaign asset with permanent life
RAQS becomes an annual or biennial property

12 / 13 رقص

Pakistani fashion is not asking
for a seat at the table.
It is setting its own.
